questions.specification:
A newly discovered aquatic species possesses both vertebrae and lungs but **LACKS** the ability to see ultraviolet (UV) light and does **NOT** produce amniotic eggs. Based on the cladogram, which organism is it most closely related to?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/BUEBuMQ9haqxnWKV_pae-.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires the analysis and interpretation of a provided cladogram to determine the evolutionary relationship of a hypothetical organism based on shared and derived characteristics such as vertebrae, lungs, UV vision, and amniotic eggs.

questions.specification: The disagreement between “splitters” and “lumpers” in taxonomy shows that scientific classification is…

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.2 Difficulties classifying organisms into the traditional hierarchy of taxa (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.2 Difficulties classifying organisms into the traditional hierarchy of taxa (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The 'splitters vs lumpers' debate concerns subjective decisions and interpretation when fitting organisms into taxonomic ranks, reflecting difficulties with traditional classification schemes.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.1 Diversity of organisms > A3.1.5  Difficulties distinguishing between populations and species
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.1.5  Difficulties distinguishing between populations and species
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The disagreement often hinges on where to draw boundaries between populations and species based on trait patterns, highlighting interpretive elements in classification.

options.content: sometimes revised when new evidence (e.g. DNA) becomes available
influenced by interpretation of traits and patterns of relatedness
based on different species concepts and chosen diagnostic characters
determined only by visible differences in appearance

questions.specification: Life is incredibly diverse, yet all organisms share common features.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.1 Need for classification of organisms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.1 Need for classification of organisms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is broadly about why organisms are classified and the principles behind classification, aligning with the need for classification of organisms.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.2 Difficulties classifying organisms into the traditional hierarchy of taxa (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.2 Difficulties classifying organisms into the traditional hierarchy of taxa (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) focus on how organisms are grouped into taxa and why systems change, directly matching difficulties with the traditional hierarchy of taxa and revisions to classification.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.9 Classification of all organisms using evidence from rRNA base sequences (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.9 Classification of all organisms using evidence from rRNA base sequences (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks about distinguishing the three domains of life, which is commonly based on molecular evidence (e.g., rRNA sequences) used to classify all organisms into domains.

parts.content: Outline the characteristics used to distinguish between the three domains of life.
parts.markscheme:
**Cell wall composition:**

a. Bacteria have peptidoglycan in cell walls; Archaea lack peptidoglycan; Eukarya may have cellulose/chitin or no cell walls; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Cellular organization:**

b. Bacteria and Archaea are prokaryotic (no membrane-bound nucleus); Eukarya are eukaryotic (membrane-bound nucleus and organelles); <marks>1 mark</marks>

**DNA organization:**

c. Bacteria and Archaea have circular DNA in cytoplasm; Eukarya have linear DNA in chromosomes within nucleus; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Membrane lipids:**

d. Archaea have unique branched-chain fatty acids in membranes; Bacteria and Eukarya have straight-chain fatty acids; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Ribosomal RNA sequences:**

e. distinct rRNA sequences differentiate all three domains; phylogenetic analysis shows separate evolutionary lineages; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Environmental preferences:**

f. Archaea often extremophiles (extreme environments); Bacteria diverse environments; Eukarya typically moderate conditions; <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Describe how organisms are classified into taxa.
parts.markscheme:
**Hierarchical classification system:**

a. organisms grouped based on shared characteristics/features; <marks>1 mark</marks>

b. classification follows hierarchical system with multiple levels; <marks>1 mark</marks>

c. taxonomic hierarchy: Domain → Kingdom → Phylum → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species; <marks>1 mark</marks>

d. each level becomes more specific/organisms become more similar; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Binomial nomenclature:**

e. scientific naming uses binomial nomenclature (two-part naming system); <marks>1 mark</marks>

f. format: Genus species (e.g., *Homo sapiens*); <marks>1 mark</marks>

g. genus name capitalized, species name lowercase; <marks>1 mark</marks>

h. both names italicized or underlined; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Classification principles:**

i. organisms in same genus are closely related/share recent common ancestor; <marks>1 mark</marks>

j. classification reflects evolutionary relationships; <marks>1 mark</marks>

k. standardized system enables universal scientific communication; <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain why classification systems are constantly revised.
parts.markscheme:
**Molecular evidence and new technologies:**

a. DNA sequencing and molecular analysis reveal genetic relationships not apparent from morphology alone; <marks>1 mark</marks>

b. phylogenetic analysis using molecular data shows true evolutionary relationships; <marks>1 mark</marks>

c. comparative genomics provides more accurate understanding of common ancestry; <marks>1 mark</marks>

d. advanced technologies (bioinformatics, genome sequencing) enable more detailed analysis; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**New discoveries and evidence:**

e. new species continue to be discovered, requiring classification updates; <marks>1 mark</marks>

f. organisms previously thought to be related may actually be unrelated (convergent evolution); <marks>1 mark</marks>

g. horizontal gene transfer (especially in prokaryotes) complicates traditional classification; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Scientific understanding and methodology:**

h. classification must reflect evolutionary relationships and common ancestry (phylogeny); <marks>1 mark</marks>

i. morphological similarities may be misleading due to convergent evolution; <marks>1 mark</marks>

j. scientific knowledge continuously advances, requiring system updates; <marks>1 mark</marks>

k. international scientific consensus drives classification changes; <marks>1 mark</marks>

l. classification systems aim to be natural (reflecting evolution) rather than artificial; <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 7 marks</note>

The figure shows a phylogenetic tree of land plants, Charophyta, and several green-algal clades (including Chlorophyta and Prasinodermophyta).

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ZSAVsnpd0B-DCzBPtrSi9.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b), (e) and (f) require reading a phylogenetic tree/cladogram to identify clades, interpret uncertain (dashed) relationships, and infer closest relatives.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.6 Base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.6 Base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks why molecular (gene/protein sequence) data is preferred for building phylogenetic trees, directly referencing sequence-based evidence.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.3 Advantages of classification corresponding to evolutionary relationships (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.3 Advantages of classification corresponding to evolutionary relationships (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) asks for advantages of classifying organisms by shared evolutionary ancestry (cladistics) rather than superficial similarity, matching advantages of evolutionary classification.

parts.content: Based on the phylogenetic tree, identify two major evolutionary clades that fall under Chlorophyta.
parts.markscheme:
Identify: core Chlorophyta <marks>1 mark</marks>
Identify: prasinophytes <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Do not accept Prasinodermophyta as the tree shows it as a distinct lineage outside of the Chlorophyta bracket.</note>

parts.content: Explain why molecular (gene or protein sequence) data is often preferred over morphological traits when building phylogenetic trees like this.
parts.markscheme:
Explain any two of the following:
- Molecular/sequence data are less subjective and more directly comparable than many morphological traits <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Molecular data can reduce error from convergent evolution producing similar (analogous) structures in unrelated groups <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Molecular datasets provide many more characters (e.g. many nucleotide/amino acid positions) for comparison <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award a maximum of 2 marks.</note>

parts.content: Describe two advantages of classifying organisms based on shared evolutionary ancestry (cladistics) rather than superficial similarities.
parts.markscheme:
Describe any two of the following:
- Classification by shared ancestry better reflects evolutionary history and relationships <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shared ancestry improves prediction of other shared traits (e.g. molecular/biochemical features) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cladistics helps infer the sequence/timing of trait evolution along lineages <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award a maximum of 2 marks.</note>

parts.content: Some relationships in the phylogenetic tree are marked with dashed lines. State what these indicate about the certainty of these relationships, and explain how future data might resolve them.
parts.markscheme:
State: dashed lines indicate uncertainty/low support/lack of consensus about the branching order <marks>1 mark</marks>
Explain: additional evidence (e.g. more genes/whole genomes and/or additional fossils/characters) could increase support and clarify the branching pattern <marks>1 mark</marks>
Additional acceptable explanation point: improved sampling/taxon sampling or improved analytical methods could resolve relationships by increasing statistical support <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award a maximum of 2 marks.</note>

parts.content: Using the tree, deduce which group is more closely related to land plants: Picocystophyceae or Charophyta.
parts.markscheme: Deduce: Charophyta (shares a more recent common ancestor with land plants than Picocystophyceae) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
The table shows the number of differences in the base sequences of a conserved mitochondrial gene between four different species of birds.

| | Species 1 | Species 2 | Species 3 | Species 4 | 
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | 
| **Species 1** | 0 | 7 | 24 | 42 |
| **Species 2** | 7 | 0 | 22 | 40 |
| **Species 3** | 24 | 22 | 0 | 38 |
| **Species 4** | 42 | 40 | 38 | 0 |

Which conclusion is supported by the data?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A4.1 Evolution and speciation > A4.1.2 Evidence for evolution from base sequences in DNA or RNA and amino acid sequences
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.1.2 Evidence for evolution from base sequences in DNA or RNA and amino acid sequences
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for conclusions about relatedness/common ancestry based on numbers of base-sequence differences in a conserved mitochondrial gene—molecular evidence for evolution from DNA/RNA sequences.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.6 Base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.6 Base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses base sequences of a gene to infer evolutionary relationships (sequence comparison), which aligns with the cladistics-focused subtopic on using gene/protein sequences as evidence.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.5 Gradual accumulation of sequence differences (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.5 Gradual accumulation of sequence differences (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Interprets degree of sequence difference as reflecting time since divergence (more differences → longer time), i.e., gradual accumulation of sequence differences.

options.content: Species 4 is the common ancestor from which the other three species evolved.
options.markscheme: Not supported: sequence differences indicate relative relatedness, not which species is the ancestor.

options.content: Species 1 and 2 shared a common ancestor more recently than Species 2 and 3.
options.markscheme: Supported: Species 1–2 have 7 differences, fewer than Species 2–3 (22), indicating a more recent common ancestor.

options.content: Species 3 is more closely related to Species 4 than it is to Species 2.
options.markscheme: Not supported: Species 3–4 have 38 differences, more than Species 3–2 (22), so Species 3 is closer to Species 2.

options.content: The DNA sequences of Species 1 and Species 2 are identical.
options.markscheme: Not supported: the table shows 7 differences between Species 1 and Species 2, so they are not identical.

questions.specification:
In the cladogram of vertebrate groups, which of the following groups shares the most recent common ancestor with Rodents?


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/69db17b3-8a4a-48e6-be16-987bf1ae790f)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which vertebrate group shares the most recent common ancestor with rodents using a cladogram, directly testing interpretation/analysis of cladograms.

options.markscheme: Primates and Rodents are shown as sister taxa in this cladogram, meaning they share a more recent common ancestor with each other than with any other group listed.
Primates and Rodents are shown as sister taxa in this cladogram, meaning they share a more recent common ancestor with each other than with any other group listed.
Amphibians share a common ancestor with Rodents, but it is further back in time (at the base of the tetrapod clade) compared to the ancestor shared with Primates.
Ray-finned fish share a common ancestor with Rodents at the base of the Osteichthyes (bony fish) clade, which is more distant than the ancestor shared with Amphibians or Primates.

questions.specification:
The table below shows the number of differences in the DNA base sequences of a conserved gene between four different species.

| | Species A | Species B | Species C | Species D |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Species A** | — | 22 | 8 | 34 |
| **Species B** | 22 | — | 25 | 11 |
| **Species C** | 8 | 25 | — | 31 |
| **Species D** | 34 | 11 | 31 | — |

Based on the data provided, which pair of species likely shares the most recent common ancestor?

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Species B and D differ by 11 bases, which is not the smallest difference in the table.
Incorrect. Species A and B differ by 22 bases, which is not the smallest difference in the table.
Correct. Species A and C have the fewest base differences (8), indicating the most recent common ancestor.
Incorrect. Species C and D differ by 31 bases, which is not the smallest difference in the table.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.8 Using cladistics to investigate evolutionary relationships (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.8 Using cladistics to investigate evolutionary relationships (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on the methodology of cladistics (identifying and using an outgroup), which is essential for investigating evolutionary relationships, establishing character polarity, and rooting trees.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Identifying an outgroup is a fundamental skill in the analysis and construction of cladograms to distinguish between ancestral and derived traits.

parts.content: Outline how scientists identify an outgroup for use in a cladogram.
parts.markscheme:
- Select a taxon that is closely related to the ingroup but not a member of it <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Choice is supported by independent evidence (for example, fossil record and/or molecular (DNA/protein) data) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outgroup shares a common ancestor with all members of the ingroup <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outgroup represents a lineage that diverged earlier than the ingroup lineages being studied <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outgroup is expected to show ancestral character states / lack the derived traits (synapomorphies) used to define the ingroup <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the importance of identifying an outgroup in a cladogram.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows the cladogram to be rooted, giving a direction to evolutionary change <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps determine character polarity / distinguishes ancestral from derived character states <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characters shared by outgroup and ingroup inferred to be ancestral (plesiomorphies) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characters present in ingroup but absent in outgroup inferred to be derived (synapomorphies) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides a reference/baseline to assess patterns of divergence and relatedness within the ingroup <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how outgroups are used to infer evolutionary relationships and character evolution when constructing and interpreting cladograms.
parts.markscheme:
- Rooting with an outgroup identifies the basal node and establishes the most recent common ancestor of the ingroup <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outgroup comparison determines which character state is ancestral versus derived for each character <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Derived states shared by multiple ingroup taxa (synapomorphies) are used to define clades/monophyletic groups <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ancestral states (plesiomorphies) are not used to define clades because they may be widespread/shared beyond the ingroup <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mapping characters onto the rooted tree allows inference of the sequence of character changes along branches <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables identification of homoplasy (convergent evolution/parallel evolution/reversals) when a derived trait appears in taxa not sharing the most recent common ancestor for that trait <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps evaluate alternative trees by favoring arrangements that require fewer independent character-state changes (parsimony) and/or better fit the character evidence once polarity is set <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
The table below shows the percentage similarity of DNA sequences between four different species.

| Species   | Species A | Species B | Species C | Species D |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Species A | 100%      | 92%       | 75%       | 65%       |
| Species B | 92%       | 100%      | 78%       | 70%       |
| Species C | 75%       | 78%       | 100%      | 85%       |
| Species D | 65%       | 70%       | 85%       | 100%      |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A4.1 Evolution and speciation > A4.1.2 Evidence for evolution from base sequences in DNA or RNA and amino acid sequences
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.1.2 Evidence for evolution from base sequences in DNA or RNA and amino acid sequences
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) uses percentage DNA sequence similarity to infer which species are most closely related—this is evidence for evolutionary relationships from base sequences.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.6 Base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.6 Base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks how molecular (DNA) sequence data are used to revise classification systems, aligning taxa with sequence-based evidence (HL cladistics context).

parts.content: Based on the data, which two species are most closely related? Justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- Species A and Species B are the most closely related because they share 92% DNA similarity, which is the highest similarity score in the table (excluding identical comparisons). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High DNA similarity suggests a more recent common ancestor / fewer mutations separating them. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable justification points (award credit for any valid justification):
- Use of molecular homology: higher sequence similarity indicates closer evolutionary relationship. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clearly states that comparisons along the diagonal (100%) are self-comparisons and are excluded. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how molecular data, such as DNA similarities, can be used to revise traditional classification systems.
parts.markscheme:
Award any two of the following points:
- DNA/protein sequence comparisons can reveal unexpected close relationships not apparent from morphology. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Molecular data can correct misclassifications caused by convergent evolution (similar appearance but not closely related). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Molecular evidence is used to construct/adjust phylogenetic trees (cladistics) and redefine taxa to reflect common ancestry. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Maximum <marks>2 marks</marks>.

questions.specification:
Based on the cladogram showing five plant species (P, Q, R, S, and T), which of the following is a valid deduction regarding their ancestry?

![cladogram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-design-technology-7969d38e-2b8c-4e30-a966-8097cc59a774-1771907030553/f476307d-46fe-4624-bed3-cdfecd0c472c.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires the interpretation and analysis of a provided cladogram to determine evolutionary relationships between specific species based on their branching patterns.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.4 Clades as groups of organisms with common ancestry and shared characteristics (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.4 Clades as groups of organisms with common ancestry and shared characteristics (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The core concept being tested is that nodes in a cladogram represent common ancestors and that proximity in branching indicates how recently those species shared a common ancestor, which defines clades.

options.content: Species S and T share a more recent common ancestor than species R and S.
options.markscheme: Correct. In a cladogram, nodes further from the root represent more recent common ancestors. Species S and T are sister taxa and share a more recent divergence point than the node connecting R to the group (S, T).

options.content: Species Q is the direct ancestor of species R, S, and T.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Cladograms show sister group relationships and common ancestry; species at the terminal tips (extant species) are not ancestors of one another.

options.content: Species P is the most advanced species because it branched off first.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Early branching does not imply being "more advanced." All species shown have undergone evolutionary change for the same total amount of time from the common root.

options.content: Species R is more closely related to species Q than to species S.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Species R shares a more recent common ancestor with species S than it does with species Q.

questions.specification:
The diagram shows a cladogram of vertebrates. What do the nodes on the cladogram represent?

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8f1524b5-b794-4800-94a9-0153a1da3893)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.7 Analysing cladograms (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks for the interpretation of a component (the node) of a provided cladogram, which falls directly under the skill of analyzing cladograms.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A3.2 Classification and cladistics (HL) > A3.2.4 Clades as groups of organisms with common ancestry and shared characteristics (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.4 Clades as groups of organisms with common ancestry and shared characteristics (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This topic defines clades based on common ancestry. Understanding that a node represents a common ancestor is fundamental to the definition of a clade and the branching structure of these diagrams.

options.markscheme: While speciation occurs at these points, nodes specifically represent the common ancestor from which the lineages diverged.
In cladistics, each node represents the most recent common ancestor of the lineages that branch from it.
Cladograms represent evolutionary relationships based on shared characteristics, not geographical movement.
Mutation rates are used to estimate the time of divergence (molecular clocks), but the nodes themselves represent the common ancestors.
